name: MIT Shaping the Future of Work
notes: MIT Shaping the Future of Work Initiative, co-led by Daron Acemoglu, David Autor, and Simon Johnson, studies how technology — particularly AI — shapes labor markets, wages, and inequality. A key academic source arguing for pro-worker AI policy, featuring prominently in congressional testimony and policy debates on AI''s employment impact.

name: MIT CSAIL
notes: MIT's Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory, directed by Daniela Rus, is one of the world's largest academic AI research labs. In 2025 CSAIL submitted formal AI Action Plan recommendations to the White House, advocating for sustained federal investment in basic AI research and maintaining U.S. strategic leadership.

name: MIT GOV/LAB
notes: MIT political science lab founded by Lily Tsai in 2014. Develops and tests innovations in citizen engagement and government accountability. Active AI work includes research on generative AI and democracy, a deliberation.io online platform, and a mini-syllabus on AI/ML and governance. Interested in the impact of AI on trust between citizens and governments.

name: MIT Media Lab
notes: MIT Media Lab is an interdisciplinary research lab at MIT funded primarily by 80+ corporate sponsors (~$45M/year budget). In 2025 it launched the AHA (Advancing Humans with AI) program studying human-AI interaction and promoting AI design that preserves human agency. It collaborates with OpenAI and publishes empirical research on AI's cognitive and social effects.

name: Princeton CITP
notes: Princeton University's Center for Information Technology Policy (CITP) is a leading interdisciplinary research center on technology and public policy. Under director Arvind Narayanan, it publishes the 'AI Snake Oil' critique of AI hype, runs AI Policy Precepts briefings for federal officials, and operates the Freedom to Tinker blog.

name: Harvard Berkman Klein Center
notes: Harvard research center on internet and society. Active AI governance research covering agentic AI, algorithmic accountability, and LLM transparency. Funded by foundations including MacArthur and Knight; no current corporate funding.

name: UW Tech Policy Lab
notes: University of Washington Tech Policy Lab is an interdisciplinary research unit bridging the UW School of Law, the Information School, and the Paul G. Allen School of Computer Science. Co-founded by Ryan Calo, it produces research on robotics law, algorithmic accountability, and emerging-technology policy, and is an active voice in Pacific Northwest tech-policy discourse.

name: Creative Destruction Lab
notes: Seed-stage science and technology accelerator founded 2012 at Rotman School of Management, U of Toronto, by Ajay Agrawal, Joshua Gans, and Avi Goldfarb. Pioneered the first university AI startup stream; now operates 30+ global programs. CDL companies have created $51B+ CAD in equity value.

name: EconTAI
notes: EconTAI (Economics of Transformative AI) is a University of Virginia research group led by economist Anton Korinek focused on the macroeconomic, distributional, and welfare implications of transformative AI, including automation of cognitive labor, wage dynamics, and post-AGI economic design.

name: Roosevelt Institute
notes: Progressive economic policy think tank with Nobel laureate Joseph Stiglitz as Chief Economist. AI work focuses on antitrust frameworks for emerging AI markets, worker rights in AI deployment, and democratic governance of automation. Argues labor voice must be central to technology policy; affiliated scholar Ganesh Sitaraman covers industrial policy.

name: Windfall Trust
notes: Windfall Trust is a research and advocacy project focused on the economic redistribution of gains from transformative AI, exploring mechanisms (equity stakes, dividends, public ownership) by which society could share in the windfall generated by advanced AI systems. Aligned with the post-labor-economics research community.

name: Collective Intelligence Project
notes: Nonprofit incubator for participatory AI governance models, co-founded by Divya Siddarth and Saffron Huang. Runs 'alignment assemblies' and community models to embed public input into AI development. Partnered with Anthropic, OpenAI, UK Frontier AI Task Force, and Taiwan's Digital Ministry. Obtained independent 501(c)3 status in 2024.

name: Plurality Institute
notes: Plurality Institute is a research and convening organization developing ''plural technology'' — tools and governance mechanisms that support diverse, decentralized collaboration. Associated with Glen Weyl''s research agenda (also linked to RadicalxChange) and engages AI policy from a democratic-infrastructure and pluralism lens rather than safety/alignment.

name: RadicalxChange
notes: RadicalxChange is a movement and nonprofit founded by economist Glen Weyl to advance mechanism-design innovations for democracy and markets — quadratic voting, quadratic funding, partial common ownership, and related tools. Active in AI policy discussions focused on collective decision-making, data dignity, and democratic governance of AI systems.

name: Taiwan Ministry of Digital Affairs
notes: Taiwan''s Ministry of Digital Affairs (MoDA), established August 2022, is the cabinet-level ministry responsible for digital development, cybersecurity, and AI policy in Taiwan. Audrey Tang served as its first minister (2022–2024) and made it a widely cited example of participatory digital governance, open-source civic tech, and democratic AI policymaking.

name: David Autor
notes: Co-authored influential 2026 Hamilton Project report 'Building Pro-Worker Artificial Intelligence' defining framework for AI that augments rather than replaces human expertise. Served on National Academy committee for 2024 report 'Artificial Intelligence and the Future of Work.' Recognized as 'academic voice of the American worker' by Economist magazine and received 2019 Carnegie Fellowship for work on globalization and technology impacts on labor markets.

name: Lawrence Katz
notes: Leading labor economist studying AI's impact on employment through the 'Race Between Education and Technology' framework. His research shows AI may substitute for entry-level workers while complementing experienced workers with tacit knowledge. Named 2025 Citation Laureate alongside David Autor for influential work on wages, inequality, and technological change. Co-authored influential book 'The Race between Education and Technology' (2008) with Claudia Goldin documenting how educational expansion affects inequality.

name: Joseph Stiglitz
notes: Warns of AI bubble that could burst while simultaneously displacing workers, creating worst-case scenario without proper retraining infrastructure. Distinguishes between 'AI' (replacement) and 'IA' (intelligence assisting), advocating for latter approach. Signed Global Call for AI Red Lines in 2026. Predicts AI will primarily impact routine white-collar jobs while augmenting rather than replacing roles in education, healthcare, and skilled trades.

name: Ajay Agrawal
notes: Co-authored influential books 'Prediction Machines' (2018) and 'Power and Prediction' (2022) framing AI as primarily a prediction technology with economic implications. Founded Creative Destruction Lab, one of the world's largest seed-stage programs for science-based companies with over 600 startups admitted in 2022. Member of the Order of Canada (2022) and recipient of University of Toronto's President's Impact Award (2023). Advocates for viewing AI through economic lens of falling prediction costs rather than focusing on AGI scenarios.

name: Joshua Gans
notes: Published multiple NBER working papers on AI regulation including 'Can Socially-Minded Governance Control the AGI Beast?' (2024) concluding that socially-minded entities cannot minimize harm from unrestricted AGI products. Provides expert witness testimony in antitrust cases and has equity/advisory relationships with startup firms. Argues that ASI is unlikely to kill humans because we find AI economically valuable and wouldn't threaten its existence.

name: Daniel Susskind
notes: Co-authored 'The Future of the Professions' (2015) with father Richard Susskind examining technology's impact on professional work. His 2026 paper 'What Will Remain for People to Do?' explores which jobs might survive AGI, identifying 'general equilibrium limits', 'preference limits', and 'moral limits'. Previously worked in British government including Prime Minister's Strategy Unit and Cabinet Office. His TED Talk on the future of work has over 1.5 million views.

name: Fiona Scott Morton
notes: Co-authored influential paper 'Cheap Goods for Everyone? The Impact of Market Power in Artificial Intelligence on Welfare and Inequality' with Susan Athey (2025). Warns that AI monopolists could create scenario where wages fall to zero while goods remain expensive. Has consulted for Microsoft on competition topics unrelated to AI and regularly advises governments on confidential antitrust cases.

name: Betsey Stevenson
notes: Author of 'What's There to Fear in a World with Transformative AI? With the Right Policy, Nothing' (2025), proposing three key policy areas: managing job transformation speed, implementing digital dividends as returns on collective data contributions, and building social trust. Served on Obama's Council of Economic Advisers and as Labor Department Chief Economist. Co-hosts 'Think Like an Economist' podcast and frequently appears in media discussing AI's economic impacts.

name: Ioana Marinescu
notes: Testified before Congress on labor market competition (October 2019) and served as Principal Economist at DOJ Antitrust Division (2022-2024), helping integrate labor market analysis into 2023 Merger Guidelines. Published 'Resilient by Design: Dual Safety Nets for Workers in the AI Economy' (2025) proposing AI Adjustment Insurance and Digital Dividend programs. Co-authored economic modeling work on AI's wage and output impacts with complementarity between physical and intelligence sectors.

name: Ramin Toloui
notes: Designed and taught one of the first university courses on AI's economic impact at Stanford. In his 'Digitalist Papers' essay, he created a framework analyzing four potential AI futures: Titan's Dominion, Copilot Empire, Disruption Swarm, and Promethean Fire. As Assistant Secretary of State (2022-2024), he represented State Department on CFIUS and developed outbound investment security programs to protect AI technologies. His testimony shows focus on using AI to modernize government operations while addressing national security implications.

name: Neil Thompson
notes: Co-authored the comprehensive AI Risk Repository (August 2024) cataloguing 777 AI risks from 43 taxonomies, and developed AI Risk Mitigation Taxonomy (December 2025) organizing 831 mitigation strategies. Has advised businesses and government on Moore's Law and computing futures, previously worked at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, Bain & Company, UN, and World Bank. Warns that smarter AI systems can more easily bypass controls and emphasizes the economic constraints of deep learning scaling.

name: Susan Athey
notes: Served as Chief Economist at DOJ Antitrust Division 2022-2024, focusing on AI and tech competition policy. Currently advises World Bank on AI implications for developing countries. Keynote speaker at 2024 IMF Statistical Forum on AI's economic impact. Research focuses on economics of digitization, causal inference with AI, and social impact applications of technology.

name: Jason Furman
notes: Served as Obama's chief economist and played major role in economic policies 2013-2017. Co-authored 'AI and the Economy' (NBER 2018) and gave influential 2016 speech 'Is This Time Different?' arguing AI resembles previous technological advances. Currently consults for OpenAI and chaired UK's Digital Competition Expert Panel, leading to establishment of Digital Markets Unit. Advocates six principles for AI regulation including application-specific approaches and comparing AI performance to imperfect humans rather than perfection.

name: Avi Goldfarb
notes: Goldfarb testified before the U.S. Senate Judiciary Committee in May 2019 on digital advertising and data privacy, arguing that privacy regulation often creates trade-offs with innovation and competition. His research with Catherine Tucker found European privacy regulations reduced online advertising effectiveness by 65%. He co-authored influential books 'Prediction Machines' (2018) and 'Power and Prediction' that frame AI economically as cheap prediction technology, widely cited in policy discussions.

name: Andrew Ng
notes: Testified before U.S. Senate AI Insight Forum arguing against regulating AI technology itself while supporting application-specific regulations. Publicly opposed California's SB 1047 and the White House's Defense Production Act use for AI regulation. Coined the term 'agentic AI' in 2024 and has been vocal about dismissing AI extinction risks as implausible while supporting responsible AI development.

name: Daniela Rus
notes: Co-authored 2020 MIT report concluding AI mass unemployment fears are unlikely to be realized, instead predicting new job creation. Focuses on 'physical AI' - AI systems that understand and interact with the physical world. Recently received 2025 IEEE Edison Medal for sustained leadership in modern robotics. Emphasizes AI as tool for human augmentation rather than replacement, advocating for policy frameworks that smooth workforce transitions.

name: Shoshana Zuboff
notes: Coined the term 'surveillance capitalism' in her 2019 book. Testified before Congress in February 2022 on privacy law. Has spent over 40 years studying digital transformation and warns that AI represents surveillance capitalism's continued evolution and expansion. Argues for 'epistemic rights' as fundamental to human autonomy in the digital age.

name: Frank Pasquale
notes: Served on U.S. National AI Advisory Committee (2022-2024) and chaired National Committee on Vital and Health Statistics Subcommittee on Privacy, Confidentiality, and Security (2019-2021). Ranked third most-cited U.S. legal scholar in Law & Technology field (2016-2020). Co-authored 'Licensing High-Risk Artificial Intelligence: Toward Ex Ante Justification for a Disruptive Technology' proposing regulatory pre-approval for high-risk AI systems. His work focuses on algorithmic accountability and has influenced AI law and policy discussions globally.

name: Ryan Calo
notes: Testified before US Senate on AI and privacy policy multiple times. Co-authored 2016 AI100 report 'Artificial Intelligence and Life in 2030.' Published influential 2017 'AI Policy Primer and Roadmap' in UC Davis Law Review. Testified July 2024 before Senate Commerce Committee on AI privacy threats, warning about the 'inference economy' where AI derives intimate information from available data.

name: Woodrow Hartzog
notes: Co-authored 'How AI Destroys Institutions' arguing AI systems erode democratic institutions through isolation, automation, and removal of human decision-making. Testified before Senate Judiciary Committee in September 2023 advocating for moving beyond 'AI half-measures.' Published extensively on privacy law including 'Privacy's Blueprint' (2018) and co-authored 'The Great Scrape' on AI data collection practices. Served on Massachusetts Special Commission on Facial Recognition.

name: Danielle Allen
notes: Co-authored 'A Roadmap for Governing AI: Technology Governance and Power-Sharing Liberalism' (2025) proposing 17 specific AI governance recommendations. Advocates for 'power-sharing liberalism' framework that prioritizes human flourishing and democratic participation over purely technocratic approaches. Has testified and advised on AI policy while directing Harvard's Allen Lab for Democracy Renovation, focusing on using AI to enhance rather than replace democratic processes.

name: Nathaniel Persily
notes: Leading scholar on AI's impact on democracy who argues that panic over AI could harm democracy more than AI itself. Co-authored 'Social Media and Democracy' (2020) and teaches 'Governing Artificial Intelligence: Law, Policy, and Institutions' at Stanford. Recently published research comparing generative AI regulation to social media regulation, advocating for transparency, trust and safety investments, and global cooperation. Awarded Guggenheim Fellowship in 2020 for work on technology's impact on political communication.

name: Eugene Volokh
notes: Leading First Amendment scholar who has written extensively on AI and free speech, including 'Freedom of Speech and AI Output' (2023) and 'Large Libel Models? Liability for AI Output' (2023). Argues that Section 230 protections don't apply to AI-generated content since it's created by the companies themselves, not third parties. Has testified as expert witness and provided analysis on California's AI training data disclosure law (AB 2013), finding it likely constitutional under First Amendment commercial speech doctrine.

name: Henry Farrell
notes: Co-authored influential Science paper 'Large AI models are cultural and social technologies' (March 2025) arguing against AGI narratives. Published 'AI as Governance' in Annual Review of Political Science (2025) examining AI's relationship to governance structures. Frequently critiques both AI 'doomers' and 'boomers' for missing real policy challenges. Won 2019 Friedrich Schiedel Prize for Politics and Technology.

name: Hélène Landemore
notes: Leading expert on democratic theory who has extended her work on citizen assemblies to AI governance. Served as advisor to OpenAI's Democratic Inputs to AI program in 2023 and received $1 million grant from Eric Schmidt Foundation for AI and democracy project with John Tasioulas. Author of 'Open Democracy' and advocates for 'politics without politicians' through sortition-based citizen rule.

name: Zeynep Tufekci
notes: Delivered keynote 'Are We Having the Wrong Nightmares About AI?' at NeurIPS 2025, arguing against AGI fixation in favor of addressing 'Artificial Good-Enough Intelligence' that breaks societal trust mechanisms. Co-authored 2025 paper on dimensional governance for AI systems. Known for early accurate predictions on technology impacts, including COVID-19 mask effectiveness and social media's democratic risks.

name: Lily Tsai
notes: Founded MIT GOV/LAB in 2014 focusing on citizen engagement and government accountability. Key contributor to The Digitalist Papers (2024) examining AI's impact on democratic societies. Research spans governance in developing contexts, particularly Asia and Africa, with emphasis on how technology can enhance rather than replace democratic participation. Advocates for 'reserved sociability' in digital civic platforms and human-centric AI governance.

name: E. Glen Weyl
notes: Co-authored influential 2024 paper 'Plurality: The Future of Collaborative Technology and Democracy' with Taiwan's Digital Minister Audrey Tang. Invented quadratic voting mechanism and quadratic funding. Led Microsoft's relationship with OpenAI as part of Office of the CTO team. Co-authored 2024 Journal of Democracy article 'The Real Dangers of Generative AI' warning of twin risks of democratic collapse and power concentration.

name: Gary Marcus
notes: Testified before U.S. Senate Judiciary Committee in May 2023 advocating for AI regulation. Consistently argues that current LLMs have fundamental limitations in reasoning, generalization, and reliability that scaling won't solve. Has been a prominent AI safety critic since 2018, warning against premature deployment of unreliable systems. Co-authored 'Rebooting AI' (2019), listed as Forbes Must-Read book on AI.

name: Noam Chomsky
notes: Chomsky is highly critical of current AI approaches, particularly Large Language Models, arguing they lack genuine understanding and cannot tell us anything meaningful about human cognition or language. He distinguishes between AI engineering (which can be useful) and AI science (which he views as fundamentally flawed). His critique is grounded in his transformational-generative grammar theory and concerns about corporate influence over AI development.

name: Alison Gopnik
notes: Leading developmental psychologist who applies her research on children's learning to critique current AI systems, particularly LLMs. Argues AI systems are 'cultural technologies' like printing presses rather than intelligent agents, and that true AI intelligence would require embodied interaction with the world. Has written extensively for major publications including Wall Street Journal and New York Times, and co-authored papers on AI as cultural technology with political scientists Henry Farrell and James Evans.

name: Divya Siddarth
notes: Founded Collective Intelligence Project to develop democratic governance models for AI. Led alignment assemblies with Taiwan's Digital Ministry that informed deepfake legislation. Co-authored Harvard Law Review piece on co-governance for AI regulation. Named to TIME100 AI list and served as TED speaker on AI and democracy.

name: Audrey Tang
notes: Led Taiwan's response to deepfake scams in 2024 using citizen assemblies with AI facilitation, resulting in new anti-fraud legislation. Pioneered 'humor over rumor' strategy during COVID-19. Developed vTaiwan platform for digital democracy and civic participation. Currently serves as Taiwan's Cyber Ambassador and Senior Fellow at Project Liberty Institute working on bridging algorithms for social media.

name: Saffron Huang
notes: Co-authored research on AI's societal impacts including work transformation studies at Anthropic and papers on interactive AI evaluations. Led democratic governance experiments including collective constitutional AI project with 1,000 Americans in 2023. Has published extensively on AI governance, digital commons, and the need for pre-distribution rather than redistribution approaches to AI's economic benefits. Currently researches how AI systems impact human behavior through extended interactions.

name: Marietje Schaake
notes: Served as Chair of Working Group on Internal Risk Management for EU AI Act Code of Practice in 2025. Co-authored white paper on Multilateral AI Research Institute (MAIRI) with Stanford colleagues in 2022. Member of UN High-level Advisory Body on AI Executive Committee. Argues that concentration of corporate power is the single greatest challenge in AI governance and prevents independent investigation of AI systems.

name: Samantha Bradshaw
notes: Leading expert on computational propaganda and disinformation who authored influential Oxford Internet Institute reports tracking organized social media manipulation in 70+ countries. Her research on 'identity-based disinformation' examines how racism, sexism, and xenophobia are weaponized online to incite violence. Has provided expert testimony to government officials in the UK, Canada, and US on technology's effects on democracy, and her work has been featured in major outlets including NYT, Washington Post, and Financial Times.

name: Alvin Graylin
notes: Co-authored 'Beyond Rivalry: A US-China Policy Framework for the Age of Transformative AI' (Dec 2024) arguing against zero-sum AI competition. Believes misuse risks from bad actors outweigh AI alignment risks and advocates for Universal Basic Individual Income (UBII) as economic safety net. Published 'Our Next Reality' (Hachette Books) on AI-XR convergence and spent decade leading HTC's VR business in China.

name: Nate Soares
notes: Co-authored the 2025 New York Times bestseller 'If Anyone Builds It, Everyone Dies' arguing that superintelligent AI will cause human extinction. Has been researching AI alignment for over a decade at MIRI, founded before major AI companies existed. Previously worked at Google on Compute Engine and Microsoft on Office 2013. Argues that modern AI systems are 'grown rather than built' making them fundamentally uncontrollable.

name: Sundar Pichai
notes: CEO of Google since 2015 and Alphabet since 2019. Born in Chennai, India, earned bachelor's in metallurgy from IIT Kharagpur (1993), master's from Stanford (1995), MBA from Wharton (2002). Joined Google in 2004, led development of Chrome browser and Android. Announced $15 billion investment in India AI infrastructure including hub in Visakhapatnam. Became billionaire in 2025, earned $226 million in 2022. Led Google through major AI race with launch of Bard/Gemini chatbot.

name: Satya Nadella
notes: Led Microsoft's $13B+ investment in OpenAI partnership since 2019. Established joint Microsoft-OpenAI Deployment Safety Board in 2021 for pre-release safety reviews. Published 'Governing AI: A Blueprint for the Future' (2023) outlining five-point regulatory framework. Under his leadership, Microsoft has nearly 350 employees working on responsible AI. Made voluntary commitments at 2023 White House AI Safety Summit including annual transparency reporting and pre-deployment safety testing.

name: Tristan Harris
notes: Former Google Design Ethicist who authored viral internal presentation 'A Call to Minimize Distraction & Respect Users' Attention' (2013). Featured prominently in Netflix's 'The Social Dilemma' documentary (2020). Named to Time 100 Next Leaders Shaping 2021 and Rolling Stone's 25 People Changing the Future. Delivered major TED Talk 'Why AI is Our Ultimate Test and Greatest Invitation' (April 2025). Co-hosts 'Your Undivided Attention' podcast. Studied Computer Science at Stanford under Terry Winograd with focus on Human-Computer Interaction.

name: Aza Raskin
notes: Co-founder of Center for Humane Technology (2018) and Earth Species Project (2017), focusing on AI-animal communication translation. Subject and architect of Emmy-winning Netflix documentary 'The Social Dilemma' (2020). Created infinite scroll technology while at Mozilla. Trained as mathematician and dark matter physicist at University of Chicago (BS) and Caltech (PhD program). Son of Macintosh co-creator Jef Raskin. Co-hosts 'Your Undivided Attention' podcast. Featured on Oprah's AI special (2024). Former head of UX at Mozilla Labs, founded multiple companies including Humanized (acquired by Mozilla), Songza, and Massive Health (acquired by Jawbone). Named FastCompany Master of Design and Forbes 30-under-30.

name: Amy Klobuchar
notes: Received Ukrainian Order of Merit, First Class from President Zelenskyy (2022). Author of 'Antitrust' (New York Times Best Seller). Graduated magna cum laude from Yale University (1982) and University of Chicago Law School with high honors (1985). Led bipartisan effort that resulted in first major U.S. AI harm legislation with Take It Down Act passage. Chair of Senate Rules Committee oversaw three bipartisan AI election bills passage in May 2024. Running for Minnesota Governor in 2026 election.

name: Ron Wyden
notes: Long-serving Senator since 1996, previously served in House 1981-1996. Co-founded Oregon Gray Panthers advocacy group and served as director of Oregon Legal Services for the Elderly (1977-1979). Holds JD from University of Oregon School of Law (1974) and BA from Stanford University. Known as leading privacy advocate who has held over 1,080 town hall meetings across Oregon's 36 counties annually. Has been warning for nearly a decade about government purchase of commercial surveillance data, calling it 'just as sensitive as information the government collects directly.' Currently fighting Trump administration's ban on Anthropic, vowing to 'pull out all the stops' and seek bipartisan support.

name: Marco Rubio
notes: First Latino to serve as U.S. Secretary of State, unanimously confirmed January 25, 2025. Served as Vice Chair of Senate Intelligence Committee (2021-2025) with focus on AI national security and U.S.-China competition. Described as 'virtual secretary of state for Latin America' during Trump's first term. Banned from entering China due to sanctions imposed twice in 2020. JD degree from University of Miami (1996), BA from University of Florida (1993).

name: Andrej Karpathy
notes: Founded Eureka Labs (2024) focused on AI-native education after leaving Tesla where he directed Autopilot AI development (2017-2022). PhD from Stanford (2016) under Fei-Fei Li, designed influential CS231n deep learning course. Founding member of OpenAI (2015-2017). Created viral 'Neural Networks: Zero to Hero' YouTube series and educational content reaching millions. Has 500K+ Twitter followers and is considered one of the most influential AI educators globally.

name: Palmer Luckey
notes: Founded Oculus VR at age 19 in 2012, sold to Facebook for $2 billion in 2014. Fired from Facebook in 2017 after donating to a pro-Trump political group; received $100M+ severance. Founded Anduril Industries in 2017; the company raised $2.5B at a $30.5B valuation in June 2025 (Founders Fund-led, $1B check — its largest ever) and in March 2026 was raising a new round led by Thrive Capital and Andreessen Horowitz targeting a ~$60B valuation. Anduril has secured major Pentagon contracts including $967M for Advanced Battle Management Systems (2020), $1B counter-UAS contract for SOCOM (2022), and a 10-year U.S. Army enterprise contract in March 2026 (ceiling up to $20B). Received Pepperdine University's inaugural President's Award for Excellence in Freedom (2024). Net worth estimated at $3.5B by Forbes (2026).

name: Bill Gates
notes: Gates Foundation portfolio exceeds $41.8 billion with 25.55% allocated to Microsoft, reflecting concentrated bet on AI infrastructure. Led $11.8 billion OpenAI investment through Microsoft. Portfolio includes Berkshire Hathaway (26%), Waste Management (19%), and Canadian National Railway (15%). In Q3 2025, sold $8.67 billion in Microsoft shares for portfolio rebalancing. Co-founded Giving Pledge in 2010 with Warren Buffett. Awarded Presidential Medal of Freedom (2016), Padma Bhushan (2015), and Honorary Knight Commander of the British Empire (2005).

name: Laurene Powell Jobs
notes: Widow of Apple co-founder Steve Jobs with estimated net worth of $11.5 billion (Bloomberg Billionaires Index). Founded Emerson Collective in 2011, which has invested in at least nine AI startups since 2022 with funding rounds totaling over $1 billion. Early investor and founding backer of Jony Ive and Sam Altman's AI hardware device venture (io/OpenAI partnership). Earned BA in political science and BS in economics from University of Pennsylvania (1985), MBA from Stanford (1991). Received Service to America Medal Spirit of Service Award (2021) and honorary doctorate from Chiba Institute of Technology (May 2025). Previously worked at Goldman Sachs and Merrill Lynch before co-founding natural foods company Terravera in 1990s.

name: Naval Ravikant
notes: Co-founded AngelList (2010) which has facilitated over $127M in annual deal flow. Angel investor in 200+ companies including early investments in Twitter and Uber. Previously co-founded Epinions (1999) and sued Benchmark Capital in high-profile case. Author of viral 'How to Get Rich (Without Getting Lucky)' tweetstorm (2018). Born in New Delhi (1974), graduated from Stuyvesant High School and Dartmouth College.

name: Chris Smalls
notes: Founded first successful Amazon warehouse union in U.S. history (ALU, 2021-2024) after being fired for organizing COVID-19 safety walkout in March 2020. Named Time Magazine's 100 Most Influential People (2022). Served as ALU president until July 2024 when replaced by Connor Spence. Participated in Gaza Freedom Flotilla (July 2024), physically assaulted by Israeli forces during ship interception. Born July 4, 1988, former rapper who toured with Meek Mill before warehouse work.

name: Kashmir Hill
notes: Technology reporter at The New York Times since July 2019, specializing in privacy and surveillance. Author of 'Your Face Belongs to Us: A Secretive Startup's Quest to End Privacy as We Know It' investigating Clearview AI. Broke major story in January 2020 exposing Clearview AI's existence and police client base. Has extensively covered 'AI psychosis' phenomenon and people developing romantic relationships with chatbots. Conducted experiment in 2024 letting AI make all her decisions for a week. Previously worked at Gizmodo, Fusion, and Forbes covering technology and privacy issues. Graduate of Duke University and New York University journalism program.

name: Walter Isaacson
notes: Bestselling biographer of major tech figures including Steve Jobs (2011), Leonardo da Vinci (2017), Jennifer Doudna (2021), and Elon Musk (2023). Rhodes Scholar and Harvard graduate who served as Time Magazine editor and CNN chairman. Received National Humanities Medal from President Biden in 2023. Currently teaches 'The Digital Revolution' course at Tulane University and co-chairs the New Orleans Book Festival.

name: Kevin Kelly
notes: Author of multiple bestselling technology books including 'The Inevitable' (2016), 'What Technology Wants' (2010), and 'Excellent Advice for Living' (2023). Co-founded Wired magazine in 1993 and served as executive editor for seven years, during which the magazine won two National Magazine Awards. Maintains active Substack newsletter with detailed analysis of AI development and publishes Cool Tools website with 1 million monthly visitors. Known for radical technological optimism and long-term perspective on technological change.

name: Humans First
notes: Nonpartisan grassroots movement originally incubated by Center for AI Safety, now independent 501(c)(3) applicant. Cross-partisan coalition includes progressive unions (AFL-CIO affiliates), conservative groups (Congress of Christian Leaders), and prominent figures like Steve Bannon, Susan Rice, Ralph Nader. Conducting nationwide town halls across red and blue states, with directors including Amy Kremer (Women for Trump co-founder) and Alexander McCoy. Key campaign: opposing 'Big AI' political donations from 11 companies including OpenAI, Anthropic, Palantir.

name: Alliance for a Better Future
notes: Founded March 2026 by Janet Kelly (former VA Health Secretary under Gov. Youngkin) and Tim Estes (AI CEO/tech entrepreneur). Coalition of 9 conservative organizations including Heritage Foundation, Family Policy Alliance, NCOSE. Plans to spend at least $10+ million in 2026 on advocacy campaigns. Policy council chaired by Brad Littlejohn (American Compass). Launched with polling showing 88% support for AI chatbot suicide discussion bans and company liability for harmful AI advice.

name: QuitGPT
notes: Consumer boycott campaign launched February 2026 targeting OpenAI's Pentagon contracts and political donations. Claims over 4 million participants based on pledges and social media engagement. Coalition includes democracy activists, climate organizers, tech workers, and pro-democracy groups. Documented measurable impact including 295% spike in ChatGPT uninstalls and competitor app growth. Core demands include ending OpenAI executive donations to Trump/Republicans and refusing military AI contracts for surveillance and autonomous weapons.

name: Stanford University
notes: Stanford University was founded in 1885 by Leland and Jane Stanford as a tribute to the memory of their only child, Leland Stanford Jr. The university officially opened in 1891 on the Stanfords' former Palo Alto farm. Stanford is organized around seven schools of study on an 8,180-acre campus, one of the largest in the nation. The university houses multiple AI-focused institutions including the Stanford Artificial Intelligence Laboratory (SAIL), founded in 1962, and the Stanford Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence (HAI), founded in 2019.

name: Massachusetts Institute of Technology
notes: The Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) is a private research university in Cambridge, Massachusetts, United States, founded in 1861. MIT's mission is to advance knowledge and educate students in science, technology, and other areas of scholarship that will best serve the nation and the world in the 21st century. The Institute has 11,886 total students as of 2024-2025, including 4,535 undergraduates and 7,351 graduate students. MIT employs 11,147 people and has an annual revenue of $3.9B.

name: Harvard University
notes: Harvard University was founded in 1636 and is the oldest institution of higher learning in the United States. The university is devoted to excellence in teaching, learning, and research, and to developing leaders in many disciplines who make a difference globally. Harvard employs 12,899 people and has an annual revenue of $6.1B. The university operates in 134 countries including United Kingdom, India, Canada, Brazil, and China.

name: Princeton University
notes: Princeton University is chartered in 1746, making it the fourth-oldest college in the United States. The University advances learning through scholarship, research, and teaching of unsurpassed quality, with an emphasis on undergraduate and doctoral education. Princeton employs 7,123 people and has an annual revenue of $3.6B. The Princeton Laboratory for Artificial Intelligence supports and expands the scope of AI research at Princeton, adopting a new organizational model designed to accommodate the challenges of working in this fast-moving field.

name: University of Cambridge
notes: The University of Cambridge is one of the world's oldest universities and leading academic centres, established in 1209. The University comprises 31 autonomous Colleges and over 100 departments, faculties and institutions. Its 25,000 students include around 11,000 international students. The University has an annual income of £2 billion.

name: University of Oxford
notes: The University of Oxford is described as "the oldest university in the English-speaking world" and "a world-leading centre of learning, teaching and research". Oxford has announced plans to expand its artificial intelligence capabilities through a five-year collaboration with OpenAI, providing students and faculty access to research grant funding, enterprise-level security and cutting-edge AI tools. The university is ranked number one in the world in the 2025 Times Higher Education World Rankings and is at the forefront of academic disciplines including medical sciences, mathematical and physical sciences, humanities, and social sciences. Oxford admitted 3,245 students in 2024, with approximately 80.5% of undergraduate places going to UK students.

name: UC Berkeley
notes: UC Berkeley is a public research university founded in 1868 and headquartered in Berkeley, California. The university employs 13,995 people with an annual revenue of $2.4B and operates in 83 countries. UC Berkeley is a leading institution in artificial intelligence research, hosting multiple AI-focused research centers including the Berkeley Artificial Intelligence Research (BAIR) Lab, which brings together over 50 faculty and 300+ graduate students across areas like computer vision, machine learning, natural language processing, planning, control, and robotics. The university has nearly 50 graduate programs ranked in the top 10 by U.S. News 2025 rankings, including Computer Science (#2), Engineering (#3), and Public Affairs (#3).

name: New York University
notes: New York University is a private research university in New York City, United States, chartered in 1831 and founded in 1832 by Albert Gallatin. NYU is one of the largest private universities in the United States by enrollment, with a total of 51,848 enrolled students in 2021. The university has degree-granting campuses in New York, Abu Dhabi, and Shanghai, with 12 global academic centers. NYU is ranked among the top institutions for artificial intelligence research, placing no. 15 worldwide according to the Nature Index 2024 AI ranking.

name: United States Senate
notes: The United States Senate is a legislative chamber in the bicameral legislature of the United States of America, and together with the U.S. House of Representatives makes up the U.S. Congress. First convened in 1789, the composition and powers of the Senate are established in Article One of the U.S. Constitution. Each U.S. state is represented by two senators, regardless of population, who serve staggered six-year terms. The chamber of the United States Senate is located in the north wing of the Capitol, in Washington, D.C., the national capital.

name: U.S. House of Representatives
notes: The U.S. House of Representatives passed a federal budget bill that includes a controversial 10-year moratorium on states regulating artificial intelligence with a vote of 215 to 214. The House established a Bipartisan Task Force on Artificial Intelligence that delivered a comprehensive report in December 2024 with 66 findings and 89 recommendations. The House held hearings on AI topics including "Advancing America's AI Action Plan" and "Building an AI-Ready America" in January 2026. The 119th Congress was sworn-in in January 2025, with Republicans holding a majority with 220 seats and Democrats holding 215 seats.

name: California State Senate
notes: The California State Senate is the upper house of the California State Legislature, consisting of 40 members elected from districts apportioned on the basis of population, with one-half elected or re-elected every two years for four-year terms. The California State Senate employs 664 people and has an annual revenue of $143.0M, founded in 1849 and headquartered in Sacramento, California. In the 2024 elections, Democrats lost one seat for a 30 seat majority to Republicans' nine seats. The Senate has been actively considering multiple artificial intelligence-related bills in 2025-2026, including measures for AI safety standards, human oversight requirements, and protection of workers from AI encroachment.

name: New York State Assembly
notes: The New York State Assembly is known as the "People's House" in New York. Since its creation in 1777, the state Assembly has helped shape history, often leading the way with progressive action and reform. Carl E. Heastie serves as Assembly Speaker, becoming New York's first African American Assembly Speaker when elected on February 3, 2015. Following the 2024 election, Democrats maintained a 103-47 majority in the Assembly.

name: White House
notes: The White House released its National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence on March 20, 2026, outlining the Trump administration's legislative priorities for federal AI governance. The framework stems from an executive order President Donald Trump signed in December that blocked states from enforcing their own regulations around artificial intelligence. The White House established the Genesis Mission in November 2025 as a national effort to accelerate the application of AI for transformative scientific discovery focused on pressing national challenges. The administration has implemented America's AI Action Plan, which recognizes the need to invest in AI-enabled science to accelerate scientific advancement.

name: State of California
notes: The State of California has issued multiple executive orders and guidelines related to artificial intelligence under Governor Gavin Newsom, including Executive Order N-12-23 on Generative Artificial Intelligence signed on September 6, 2023. The state published GenAI Guidelines for Public Sector Procurement, Uses and Training in March 2024. Governor Newsom signed another executive order in 2026 to strengthen AI protections and responsible use while the Trump administration was rolling back federal protections. California leads the nation in AI regulations and is home to many of the largest AI companies in the world.

name: State of Texas
notes: The State of Texas signed the Texas Responsible Artificial Intelligence Governance Act (TRAIGA) into law on June 22, 2025, with an effective date of January 1, 2026. The law prohibits AI systems designed to manipulate behavior causing harm, discriminate based on protected characteristics, produce child sexual abuse material, or enable social scoring by government. The Texas Attorney General has exclusive authority to enforce TRAIGA. The state's Consolidated General Revenue Fund ended fiscal 2025 with a cash balance of $41.4 billion, an increase of $4.0 billion from fiscal 2024.

name: State of Colorado
notes: The State of Colorado enacted Senate Bill 24-205, also known as the Colorado Anti-Discrimination in AI Law (ADAI), which was signed into law on May 17, 2024. The ADAI is part of the State of Colorado's Consumer Protection Act and its provisions go into effect February 1, 2026. The Colorado Attorney General has exclusive enforcement authority for this legislation and is tasked with creating rules to implement and enforce the ADAI's requirements. History Colorado is established as a 501(c)(3) charitable organization and an agency of the State of Colorado under the Department of Higher Education.

name: State of New York
notes: The State of New York Office of Information Technology Services established the NYS-P24-001 policy for acceptable use of AI technologies by state entities, updated on March 11, 2025. Governor Kathy Hochul announced the FY 2025 State Budget which included a $275 million investment over 10 years to support Empire AI, a consortium to secure New York's position in artificial intelligence. The New York State Executive Chamber works to meet the needs of all New Yorkers by advancing the Governor's policy initiatives and agenda, as well as overseeing government operations. The State of New York has various departments including the Department of State, which is one of the oldest and most diverse agencies in state government.

name: State of Ohio
notes: Ohio became the 17th state in the Union in 1803, the same year as the Louisiana Purchase. The Ohio Department of Job and Family Services received the Merrill Baumgardner Award from the Washington-based National Association of State Workforce Agencies, which honors states that demonstrate excellence in information technology and set benchmarks nationwide. Ohio has released a new policy that includes guidance and requirements for how Ohio state government workers can use AI and generative AI for work within their agencies. Governor Mike DeWine has signed Ohio's Fiscal Year 2024-2025 operating budget bill into law.

name: Office of the Governor of Indiana
notes: The Office of the Governor of Indiana is the chief executive office of the state under Article 5 of the Indiana Constitution. The current Governor is Mike Braun, who delivered his first State of the State Address on January 29, 2025. The office operates from the Indiana Statehouse in Indianapolis and coordinates directly with the Indiana Lieutenant Governor. According to company data, the Office of the Governor of Indiana employs 14 people and is classified as a Government Administration organization.

name: Republican Party
notes: The Republican Party is confronting an unfamiliar fracture over artificial intelligence, with the battleground being AI regulation and the combatants being red-state lawmakers who want to protect their constituents versus a White House determined to clear the runway for Big Tech. Governors like Ron DeSantis and Spencer Cox are championing state-level AI regulations, focusing on a consumer 'Bill of Rights' and protecting children. The Trump administration has flipped the script, aggressively pressuring Republican state legislators to abandon bills designed to protect children and establish basic transparency requirements for AI companies. More than 50 Republican state lawmakers signed a letter to President Trump expressing deep concern by the work of officials seeking to pressure lawmakers in Utah and other states to abandon legislation aimed at mitigating risks.

name: Democratic Party
notes: The Democratic Party is the major liberal political party in the United States, sitting on the center to center-left of the political spectrum, founded in 1828, making it the world's oldest active political party. House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries announced the launch of a new House Democratic Commission on AI and the Innovation Economy that will convene throughout 2026. The Democratic National Committee (DNC) has been the heart of the Democratic Party since 1848, and is the oldest continuing political party in the United States. Ken Martin serves as the current Chairperson of the Democratic Party.

name: Google
notes: Google is an American multinational technology corporation focused on artificial intelligence and other technologies. The company was founded in 1998 by Larry Page and Sergey Brin. Sundar Pichai was appointed CEO of Google in 2015, replacing Larry Page, and in 2019, Pichai also became the CEO of Alphabet. Google is Alphabet's largest subsidiary and is a holding company for Alphabet's internet properties and interests.

name: Microsoft
notes: Microsoft Corporation is an American multinational technology conglomerate headquartered in Redmond, Washington. Founded in 1975, the company became influential in the rise of personal computers through software like Windows, and has since expanded to Internet services, cloud computing, artificial intelligence, video gaming, and other fields. Microsoft is the largest software company by revenue, one of the most valuable public companies, and one of the most valuable brands globally. Revenue was $281.7 billion, up 15 percent in fiscal 2025.

name: Meta
notes: Meta Platforms, Inc. is the developer of the Meta AI app and various AI products including Meta AI assistant built with Llama technology. The company develops and releases the Llama series of open-source large language models, with Llama 3 and Llama 4 being their latest offerings. Meta's mission is to build the future of human connection and the technology that makes it possible. The company reported revenue of $200.97 billion for full year 2025, representing a 22% year-over-year increase.

name: University of Virginia
notes: The University of Virginia is a public research university located in Charlottesville City and Albemarle County in Virginia, United States. It was founded in 1819 by Thomas Jefferson and contains his Academical Village, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The university has been a member of the Association of American Universities since 1904. UVA's Board of Visitors approved the new LaCross Institute for Ethical Artificial Intelligence in Business at UVA's Darden School of Business.

name: Vanderbilt University
notes: Vanderbilt University was founded in 1873 by Cornelius Vanderbilt, who gave $1 million to endow and build the university. The university is a private research university with approximately 6,900 undergraduates and more than 6,200 graduate and professional students. Vanderbilt has launched the Amplify Generative AI Innovation Center within the College of Connected Computing, which is co-led by Professor Jules White and Chief AI and Technology Officer Allen Karns. The university's core purpose is to create, preserve, and disseminate knowledge through teaching, research, and the arts.

name: University College London
notes: UCL was chosen as the sole UK partner in NVIDIA's Europe-wide initiative to help develop sovereign AI platforms, building on its strong existing relationship with NVIDIA and ongoing work creating a UK designed and trained large language model called BritLLM. UCL leads the EPSRC AI hub in Generative Models with £80 million investment, developing tools for responsible generative AI that will benefit the economy and society. UCL maintains strong partnerships with leading AI firms such as NVIDIA, Darktrace, Google DeepMind and Quantexa, as well as with the UK's AI Safety Institute. UCL is consistently ranked as one of the top ten universities in the world and is 9th in the 2026 QS World University Rankings.

name: Brown University
notes: Brown University is a leading nonprofit research university founded in 1764 and located in Providence, Rhode Island. The university has 7,272 undergraduate students, 3,130 graduate students, and 866 regular faculty. Brown received a $20 million grant from the U.S. National Science Foundation to lead the AI Research Institute on Interaction for AI Assistants (ARIA) focused on developing trustworthy AI assistants for mental and behavioral health applications. The university operates with a Fiscal Year 2025 budget of $1.79 billion in revenues and $1.83 billion in expenditures.

name: Columbia University
notes: Columbia University offers a Master of Science in Artificial Intelligence program that combines core AI courses in Computer Science and Engineering with comprehensive concentrations. The university leads a $20 million NSF-funded AI Institute for Artificial and Natural Intelligence (ARNI), collaborating with academic and industry partners. Columbia operates the Center of Artificial Intelligence Technology in collaboration with Amazon, guided by the Engineering for Humanity vision. The university was founded as King's College in 1754 and renamed Columbia University in the City of New York in 1896.

name: University of Washington
notes: The University of Washington is comprised of three campuses: the Seattle campus with sixteen schools and colleges, plus the Bothell and Tacoma campuses. The primary mission of the University of Washington is the preservation, advancement, and dissemination of knowledge. The UW has been recognized as the #1 most innovative public university in the world and receives more federal research dollars than any other U.S. public university. The University was founded on November 4, 1861, making it one of the oldest state-supported institutions of higher education on the Pacific coast.

name: MIT Sloan
notes: MIT Sloan is the MIT Sloan School of Management, located in Cambridge, Massachusetts, United States. The school employs 1,398 people and operates in 49 countries. Richard M. Locke is the John C Head III Dean at MIT Sloan, beginning his appointment as dean in July 2025. MIT Sloan offers various programs including MBA, Executive Education, and specialized programs like the MIT Sloan Fellows MBA Program.

name: Center for Humane Technology
notes: The Center for Humane Technology is a nonprofit organization founded in 2018 and headquartered in San Francisco, California, dedicated to radically reimagining digital infrastructure and driving a comprehensive shift toward humane technology. The organization was co-founded by Tristan Harris and Aza Raskin, with Harris previously serving as a Design Ethicist at Google where he created the viral presentation 'A Call to Minimize Distraction & Respect Users' Attention'. CHT employs 22 people and focuses on transforming the incentives that drive technology, from social media to artificial intelligence, working to catalyze systems-level change in the tech ecosystem. The organization has launched a new area of work called 'AI and What Makes Us Human' to address what new norms, legal protections, and fundamental rights are needed to preserve what makes life meaningful in the age of AI.

name: DAIR Institute
notes: The Distributed Artificial Intelligence Research Institute (or DAIR Institute) is a research institute founded by Timnit Gebru in December 2021. The institute announced itself as "an independent, community-rooted institute set to counter Big Tech's pervasive influence on the research, development and deployment of AI". In February 2023, two other members of Google's Ethical AI research group, researcher Alex Hanna and developer Dylan Baker, left Google to join DAIR. DAIR is committed to interdisciplinary and globally distributed AI research.

name: Mila
notes: Mila is the Quebec Artificial Intelligence Institute, recognized worldwide for its major contributions to AI and described as the world's largest academic research center for deep learning. The institute is a community of more than 1,400 researchers specializing in machine learning and dedicated to scientific excellence and innovation. Mila is the result of a unique collaboration between Université de Montréal and McGill University, in close collaboration with Polytechnique Montréal and HEC Montréal, and also hosts professors from several other universities. Founded by Professor Yoshua Bengio in 1993, Mila acquired the status of non-profit organization in 2018.

name: Signal
notes: Nonprofit developer of the Signal encrypted-messaging app. President Meredith Whittaker argues AI is fundamentally a surveillance technology that expands harmful data-collection business models. Signal has publicly warned that agentic AI poses an existential threat to secure messaging, and opposes mandatory message-scanning laws such as the EU Chat Control proposal.

name: Gates Foundation
notes: The Gates Foundation is an American private foundation founded by Bill Gates and Melinda French Gates, based in Seattle, Washington, launched in 2000 and reported to be the third-wealthiest charitable foundation in the world, holding $86 billion in assets as of July 31, 2025. The foundation's primary stated goals are to enhance healthcare and reduce extreme poverty across the world, and to expand educational opportunities and access to information technology in the United States. In May 2025, Bill Gates announced the foundation will spend $200 billion over the next 20 years and set a new date of 2045 to sunset its operations. The foundation's 2025 budget reached $8.74 billion, which is the largest budget in its history.

name: Emerson Collective
notes: Emerson Collective, LLC is an American company founded by Laurene Powell Jobs in 2011 in Palo Alto, California. The company invests in entrepreneurs and innovators working in education, energy and the environment, immigration, media and journalism. Emerson Collective has invested in at least nine AI startups since 2022, with involvement in AI funding rounds totaling over $1 billion. The organization operates as a limited liability company and focuses on venture capital investment and philanthropy.

name: AngelList
notes: AngelList is building the infrastructure that powers the startup economy and aims to accelerate innovation by providing startups and investors with the connected tools they need to launch and scale a startup or fund—and invest in both. AngelList reaches over $171B in assets on the platform, with over 72k investors, 13k active startups, and more than 25k funds and syndicates supported. AngelList is the parent company of AngelList Venture, AngelList Talent, and Product Hunt and has more than 20 unicorns in the portfolio. The company provides investors and innovators with the tools to grow, offering venture funds, SPVs, scout funds, and digital subscriptions.

name: Anduril Industries
notes: Defense technology company founded in 2017 by Palmer Luckey, building AI-powered autonomous weapons, surveillance systems, and battlefield software (Lattice). Valued at $30.5B in June 2025 ($2.5B Series G led by Founders Fund); in March 2026 raising a new round led by Thrive Capital and Andreessen Horowitz targeting ~$60B. In March 2026 awarded a 10-year U.S. Army enterprise contract with a ceiling of up to $20B, consolidating 120+ separate procurement actions around Lattice — the largest single contract in Anduril's history (first task order: $87M to JIATF 401 for counter-UAS C2). Sacra estimates $2.1B revenue in 2025 (up 110% YoY), projecting $4.3B in 2026. Luckey publicly opposes corporate restrictions on military AI use, arguing elected government — not corporate executives — should set AI weapons policy.

name: The New York Times
notes: The New York Times is a media organization dedicated to seeking the truth and helping people understand the world through independent journalism. The company has over 11.4 million subscribers and employs approximately 2,000 journalists in its newsroom. The New York Times has created an A.I. Initiatives team led by Editorial Director Zach Seward to leverage artificial intelligence for journalism while maintaining human oversight. The organization filed a lawsuit against Perplexity AI for unauthorized use of Times content.

name: Platformer
notes: Independent tech newsletter founded in 2020 by Casey Newton, focusing on the intersection of technology and democracy. Covers major AI labs and platforms with a critical, product-focused lens. Subscriber-funded via Ghost; Newton declined Substack advances and moved the publication off Substack in 2024 over content moderation concerns.

name: Common Sense Media
notes: Common Sense Media is an American nonprofit organization founded by Jim Steyer in 2003 that reviews and provides ratings for media and technology with the goal of providing information on their suitability for children. The organization is headquartered in San Francisco, California and is dedicated to improving the lives of kids and families by providing trustworthy information, education, and independent voice they need to thrive. Common Sense Media rates movies, TV shows, books, and more so parents can feel good about the entertainment choices they make for their kids, and offers the largest, most trusted library of independent age-based ratings and reviews. The organization also advocates publicly for child-friendly policies and laws regarding media and education, and funds research on the role of media in the lives of children.

name: Civic Signals
notes: Research initiative co-founded by Eli Pariser and Talia Stroud, originally a project of the National Conference on Citizenship and the Center for Media Engagement at UT Austin. Developed a framework of 14 design principles ("signals") for evaluating and building healthier digital public spaces, based on a 20-country YouGov survey of 20,000+ people funded by the Omidyar Network. Rebranded as New_Public in 2021 and continues work on digital public infrastructure. Advocates for treating digital spaces like physical public infrastructure (libraries, parks, town squares) with civic design principles. Jennifer Pahlka is affiliated (edge: employer).

name: Amazon Labor Union
notes: The Amazon Labor Union (ALU) was founded by warehouse workers at the JFK8 Fulfillment Center in Staten Island, New York, and is now known as Amazon Labor Union–IBT Local 1. The NLRB recently ordered Amazon.com Services to bargain with Amazon Labor Union at a fulfillment center located in Staten Island, N.Y. (JFK8 Facility). Chris Smalls is identified as the Amazon Labor Union founder who wrote a book called 'When the Revolution Comes'. The union operates as a worker-led union committed to transforming conditions at Amazon and describes itself as a fighting union built by and for Amazon workers.

name: National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER)
notes: The National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER) is a private, nonprofit organization that facilitates cutting-edge research on and analysis of major economic issues, founded in New York City in 1920 and today headquartered in Cambridge, Massachusetts. The NBER is a network of more than 1,800 academic economists, with 43 current or former NBER affiliates having been awarded the Nobel Prize in Economic Sciences, and 13 have chaired the President's Council of Economic Advisers. The organization recently conducted a study surveying nearly 6,000 top executives across the US, UK, Germany and Australia about AI's impact on employment and productivity. The current president and chief executive officer is MIT professor of economics James Poterba.

name: Google Brain
notes: Google Brain was a deep learning artificial intelligence research team that served as the sole AI branch of Google before being incorporated under the newer umbrella of Google AI, a research division at Google dedicated to artificial intelligence. Formed in 2011, it combined open-ended machine learning research with information systems and large-scale computing resources. It was merged into former Google sister company DeepMind to form Google DeepMind in April 2023. Google Brain was initially established by Google Fellow Jeff Dean and visiting Stanford professor Andrew Ng.

name: Google Research
notes: Google Research is the research division of Google, a subsidiary of Alphabet Inc. According to its official website, Google Research publishes findings, releases open-source software, and applies research results within Google products and services as well as within the wider scientific community. Google Research maintains a portfolio of research projects driven by fundamental research, new product innovation, product contribution and infrastructure goals. The organization is led by Yossi Matias, Vice President & Head of Google Research.

name: Council on Foreign Relations
notes: The Council on Foreign Relations (CFR) is a nonpartisan, independent national membership organization, think tank, educator, and publisher founded in 1921. CFR publishes Foreign Affairs magazine and generates policy-relevant ideas and analysis on the most consequential issues facing the United States and the world. The organization has 1,277 employees and an annual revenue of $77.3 million. CFR's mission is to inform U.S. engagement with the world and convenes experts and policymakers to promote informed public discussion.

name: World Economic Forum
notes: The World Economic Forum is an independent, not-for-profit organization, headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland, with offices in New York and San Francisco, United States, Beijing, China, and Tokyo, Japan. Founded in 1971 as a not-for-profit foundation, the World Economic Forum set out with a transformative point of view: that business should not only to create economic value but also to serve society and the planet. The Forum is the International Organization for Public-Private Cooperation that engages the foremost political, business and other leaders of society to shape global, regional and industry agendas. The Forum has served as an impartial, globally respected platform for cooperation for more than five decades.

name: Microsoft Research
notes: Microsoft Research is a multidisciplinary global organization working to create scientific knowledge and deliver innovation that leads to positive human impact at scale. For nearly 35 years, Microsoft Research has shaped the technology people use every day through a commitment to hiring the world's top minds and giving them autonomy, resources, and support to conduct research. Microsoft Research has research labs across the globe and mission-focused efforts in areas like scientific discovery and human health, conducting fundamental science and technology research that benefits humanity. The organization was founded in 1991, with the original Microsoft Research Plan memo serving as the catalyst for the development of the organization.

name: Facebook AI Research (FAIR)
notes: Facebook AI Research (FAIR) Paris was created in 2015 to advance the field of artificial intelligence through open research for the benefit of all. FAIR has grown into an international research organization with labs in Menlo Park, New York, Paris, Montreal, Tel Aviv, Seattle, Pittsburgh, and London. For more than a decade, Meta's Fundamental AI Research (FAIR) team has focused on advancing the state of the art in AI through open research. FAIR has applied an open model to all aspects of their work, collaborating broadly with the community by publishing cutting-edge research early and often, and open-sourcing research code, data sets, and tools like PyTorch, fastText, FAISS, and Detectron.

name: Tesla
notes: Tesla announced a joint project with xAI called Digital Optimus, also nicknamed 'Macrohard,' designed to automate complex office workflows by observing and replicating human interactions with computers. As part of this collaboration, Tesla made a $2 billion investment in xAI in 2026. Tesla's financial performance declined significantly in 2025, with profits falling 46 percent and revenue dropping for the first time in the company's history. The company's automotive revenues fell by 11 percent to $17.7 billion in Q4 2025, though energy storage business grew 25 percent.

name: Stripe
notes: Stripe was founded in 2010 by two Irish brothers, Patrick Collison (CEO) and John Collison (President), who came to the US in 2006 at ages 18 and 16 respectively. In 2025, businesses running on Stripe generated $1.9 trillion in total volume, up 34% from 2024, and equivalent to roughly 1.6% of global GDP. Stripe now powers more than 5 million businesses directly or via platforms, including 90% of the Dow Jones Industrial Average and 80% of the Nasdaq 100. The company has developed AI-focused products including the Stripe integration benchmark for evaluating AI agents' ability to build complete Stripe integrations, and the Agentic Commerce Suite for enabling businesses to sell on AI agents.

name: Palantir
notes: Palantir Technologies is a data analytics and AI company that builds software platforms for data integration and analysis, serving government and commercial clients. The company was founded in 2003 by Peter Thiel and others, with Alex Karp joining as CEO in 2004. Palantir's Maven AI system has been adopted by the Pentagon as a core military system for weapons targeting and battlefield data analysis. The company reported Q4 2025 revenue of $1.41 billion, up 70% year-over-year, with a market capitalization of approximately $100.6 billion.

name: DeepLearning.AI
notes: DeepLearning.AI was founded in 2017 by Andrew Ng, a global leader in AI. The company is an education technology company that empowers the global workforce to build an AI-powered future through world-class education, hands-on training, and a collaborative community. DeepLearning.AI is making a world-class AI education accessible to people around the globe. The company is headquartered in Mountain View, California and employs 237 people.

name: Coursera
notes: Coursera was founded by Stanford University professors Andrew Ng and Daphne Koller in 2012. The company has grown to serve 197 million registered learners as of December 31, 2025. Coursera partners with over 375 leading university and industry partners to offer courses, specializations, professional certificates, and degrees. The company went public on the New York Stock Exchange in March 2021.

name: BlackRock
notes: BlackRock is a global asset manager and technology provider with over $14 trillion in assets under management as of Q4 2025. The company has formed an AI Infrastructure Partnership with Microsoft, raising $12.5 billion toward a $30 billion goal. BlackRock has built an AI research platform dubbed 'Asimov' for use in its fundamental equity business. The company's CEO Larry Fink has framed AI as 'the most significant technology since, at least, the computer' in his 2026 annual letter to investors.

name: University of Toronto
notes: The University of Toronto is described as a world-leader in Artificial Intelligence (AI) research, including machine and deep learning, natural language processing, and computer vision. The university has established the Hinton Chair in Artificial Intelligence with $10 million in funding from Google, with U of T matching Google's support with an additional $10 million in funding. U of T ranked 21st globally in the Times Higher Education World University Rankings 2025 and is the top-ranked university in Canada. The university was founded in 1827 and has three campuses: St. George, Mississauga, and Scarborough.

name: Carnegie Mellon University
notes: Carnegie Mellon University was founded in 1900 by philanthropist Andrew Carnegie who donated $1 million to establish a school where students from all backgrounds could gain skills to uplift their careers and communities. The university was originally called Carnegie Technical Schools (1900-1912), then Carnegie Institute of Technology (1912-1967), and became Carnegie Mellon University in 1967 when Carnegie Tech merged with the Mellon Institute. Carnegie Mellon is described as the birthplace of AI over half a century ago and maintains the world's #1 ranked AI program.

name: Georgetown University
notes: Georgetown University is a Catholic and Jesuit, student-centered research university established in 1789. The university offers an online Master of Professional Studies in Artificial Intelligence Management through Georgetown SCS, which is a 30-credit program with tuition of $52,560. Georgetown has adopted Google Gemini for students, faculty and staff as part of its AI initiatives. The university has more than 22,000 undergraduate and graduate students who take classes at five locations: Main Campus, Medical Center, Law Center, School of Continuing Studies, and the university's Georgetown Qatar campus.

name: Yale University
notes: Yale University was founded in 1701 and is committed to improving the world today and for future generations through outstanding research and scholarship, education, preservation, and practice. Yale announced over $150 million in funding over five years to support faculty, students, and staff as they engage with artificial intelligence. The university is located in New Haven, Connecticut and has approximately 15,564 total students including 6,814 undergraduates and 8,750 graduate students. Yale's mission includes educating aspiring leaders worldwide who serve all sectors of society through the free exchange of ideas in an ethical, interdependent, and diverse community.

name: United Nations
notes: The United Nations is an international organization founded in 1945, currently made up of 193 Member States, with work guided by the purposes and principles contained in its founding Charter. The UN has established a High-Level Advisory Body on AI to analyze the current situation and recommend governance approaches for artificial intelligence. The General Assembly approved a $3.72 billion budget for the United Nations for 2025. The UN Charter was signed on 26 June 1945 in San Francisco and came into force on 24 October, which is now recognized as UN Day.

name: European Union
notes: The European Union is a political and economic union of 27 member states that are located primarily in Europe. The EU was established, along with its citizenship, when the Maastricht Treaty came into force in 1993, and was incorporated as an international legal juridical person upon entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon in 2009. In 2012, the EU was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize. The European Union has enacted the Artificial Intelligence Act, which is a European Union regulation concerning artificial intelligence that entered into force on 1 August 2024.

name: DARPA
notes: DARPA is a research and development agency of the United States Department of Defense responsible for the development of emerging technologies for use by the military. The agency was created on February 7, 1958, by President Dwight D. Eisenhower in response to the Soviet's launch of Sputnik 1 in 1957. DARPA comprises about 220 government employees in six technical offices, including nearly 100 program managers. The agency's annual budget is $4.122 billion (FY2024).

name: University of Michigan
notes: The University of Michigan is a public research university in Ann Arbor, Michigan, United States, founded in 1817 and is the oldest institution of higher education in the state. The university reported record total enrollment of 52,855 students for fall 2024, with undergraduate enrollment of 34,454 students. U-M has annual research volume that exceeds $1.86 billion and employs 20,482 people. The university received a record number of applications for fall 2025 with more than 115,000 prospective first-year and transfer students.

name: UCLA
notes: UCLA has an AI Innovation Initiative that is a campus-wide effort to build AI fluency, foster innovation, and drive responsible AI adoption. The initiative includes strategic partnerships with leaders like OpenAI and Google to empower faculty, staff, and students. UCLA's primary purpose as a public research university is the creation, dissemination, preservation and application of knowledge for the betterment of our global society. The university operates on a quarter system with Fall, Winter, Spring quarters and Summer sessions.

name: Johns Hopkins University
notes: Johns Hopkins University offers multiple artificial intelligence programs including an online Master of Science in Artificial Intelligence developed with expertise from the Johns Hopkins Applied Physics Lab. The university also has a Johns Hopkins Data Science and AI Institute that focuses on research and education in data science, machine learning, and artificial intelligence across diverse fields. The university's mission is to educate students and cultivate their capacity for lifelong learning, to foster independent and original research, and to bring the benefits of discovery to the world. Johns Hopkins University was founded in 1876 with the inauguration of its first president, Daniel Coit Gilman.

name: University of Louisville
notes: The University of Louisville offers multiple artificial intelligence programs including a Graduate Certificate in Artificial Intelligence in Medicine and a Master of Science in Artificial Intelligence in Medicine. The university was established in 1798 and is a state supported research university located in Kentucky's largest metropolitan area. As of Fall 2024, the university has a total student enrollment of 24,123 with faculty and staff totaling 7,231. The university operates with an annual revenue of $1.3 billion and has three campuses including the 287-acre Belknap Campus.

name: American University
notes: American University is a private federally chartered research university in Washington, D.C., chartered by Congress in 1893. The university consists of eight schools and colleges, including the Kogod School of Business, School of International Service, College of Arts and Sciences, School of Communication, School of Professional and Extended Studies, School of Public Affairs, Linda A. and H. Kent Baker School of Education, and Washington College of Law. American University has 13,019 students as of fall 2023 and offers over 160 academic programs. The Kogod School of Business has launched the Institute for Applied Artificial Intelligence (IAAI) and has been recognized for having 'the most consequential AI transformation in business education' according to Poets&Quants.

name: SUNY Albany
notes: SUNY Albany is also known as University at Albany, UAlbany, or SUNY Albany and is a public research university in Albany, New York, United States. The university was founded in 1844 and is one of four "university centers" of the State University of New York (SUNY) system. In 2024, the university enrolled 17,567 students in nine schools and colleges, which offer over 50 undergraduate majors and over 150 graduate programs. The university is classified among "R1: Doctoral Universities – Very high research spending and doctorate production".

name: USC
notes: USC is a private university founded in 1880 that is home to more than 48,000 students and over 4,400 full-time faculty. USC launched the Frontiers of Computing initiative in 2023, a $1B-plus initiative for advanced computation, quantum computing, AI and ethics as the largest, most comprehensive academic initiative in USC's history. USC has multiple AI research centers and initiatives, including the USC Center for AI in Society founded in 2016, which is a collaboration between USC's Suzanne Dworak-Peck School of Social Work and Viterbi School of Engineering. The university provides every student, faculty member, staff member and researcher with secure, enterprise-grade AI tools.

name: Wharton School
notes: The Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania was founded in 1881 as the world's first collegiate business school.
