It has more than 235 renowned professors and 5,000 undergraduate, MBA, executive MBA, and doctoral students. In April 2025, Wharton announced a new undergraduate concentration and MBA major in Artificial Intelligence for Business. The school is led by Dean Erika James.

name: Tulane University
notes: Tulane University is a private research university in New Orleans, Louisiana, United States that was founded as the Medical College of Louisiana in 1834 by a cohort of medical doctors. The institution became private under the endowments of Paul Tulane and Josephine Louise Newcomb in 1884 and 1887. Tulane has been a member of the Association of American Universities since 1958 and is classified among "R1: Doctoral Universities – Very high research activity". The university has established multiple AI-related initiatives including the Connolly Alexander Institute for Data Science, the Jurist Center for Artificial Intelligence, and the Center for Community-Engaged Artificial Intelligence.

name: U.S. Department of Transportation
notes: The U.S. Department of Transportation (DOT) is committed to safety and innovation and sees artificial intelligence (AI) as a promising capability to help achieve these aims. The Department has established an AI Compliance Plan in response to the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) Memorandum M-25-21, Accelerating Federal Use of AI through Innovation, Governance, and Public Trust. The Department was authorized in October 1966 and launched on 1 April 1967, with a mission to ensure that federal funds were effectively used to support the national transportation program. The mission of the USDOT is to ensure our nation has the safest, most efficient and modern transportation system in the world; that improves the quality of life for all American people and communities, from rural to urban, and increases the productivity and competitiveness of American workers and businesses.

name: Peter Norvig
notes: Peter Norvig is a Distinguished Education Fellow at Stanford's Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence Institute and a researcher at Google Inc. He co-authored "Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach" with Stuart Russell, which is the leading textbook in the field used in more than 1,500 universities in 135 countries. He was born on December 14, 1956, and previously served as head of NASA Ames's Computational Sciences Division where he was NASA's senior computer scientist. He co-taught an online Artificial Intelligence class that signed up 160,000 students, helping to kick off massive open online classes.

name: University of California, San Francisco
notes: The University of California, San Francisco (UCSF or UC San Francisco) is a public land-grant research university in San Francisco, California, United States that is part of the University of California system and is dedicated entirely to health science and life science. UCSF was founded as Toland Medical College in 1864, became affiliated with the University of California as its medical department in 1873, gained full administrative independence as a campus of the UC system in 1964, and gained its current name in 1970. UCSF's mission is to advance health worldwide through preeminent biomedical research, graduate-level education in the life sciences and health professions, and excellence in patient care. In 2023, UCSF received the 2nd highest research funding from the National Institutes of Health.

name: Bayesian Logic Inc.
notes: Bayesian Logic Inc. is co-founded by Nimar Arora, who serves as Chief Executive Officer, Secretary, Chief Financial Officer, and Director. Stuart Russell serves as a Director and is described as founder and Vice-President of the company. The company was officially filed as a General Corporation in California on October 24, 2011, and maintains active status. Bayesian Logic Inc. developed NET-VISA, a Bayesian seismic monitoring system for detecting nuclear explosions, which has been deployed by the CTBT organization of the U.N. and achieves around 60% reduction in missed events compared to currently deployed systems.

name: Michael Genesereth
notes: Michael Genesereth (born 1948) is an American logician and computer scientist, who is most known for his work on computational logic and applications of that work in enterprise management, computational law, and general game playing. Genesereth is professor in the Computer Science Department at Stanford University and a professor by courtesy in the Stanford Law School. His 1987 textbook on Logical Foundations of Artificial Intelligence remains one of the key references on symbolic artificial intelligence. He is the author of the influential Game Description Language(GDL) and Knowledge Interchange Format(KIF), the latter of which led to the ISO Common Logic standard.

name: Tom Cotton
notes: Tom Cotton is a U.S. Senator representing Arkansas who introduced the DATA Act of 2026, which would allow AI data center companies to bypass federal electricity regulations by building independent energy infrastructure. Cotton stated that "American dominance in artificial intelligence and other crucial emerging industries should not come at the expense of Arkansans paying higher energy costs". On November 13, 2024, Senate Republicans selected Cotton as the next conference chair, making him the third-highest ranked Republican in the Senate. Cotton previously co-introduced a resolution with Senator Chris Coons to affirm America's artificial intelligence dominance.

name: Andy Kim
notes: Andy Kim is a U.S. Senator from New Jersey who serves as a Democrat on multiple committees including Commerce, Science, and Transportation. He has been actively involved in AI-related legislation, co-introducing the Artificial Intelligence Ready Data Act with Senator Ted Budd to open government data sets for training American AI models. Kim also co-introduced the AI Talent Act with Senator Jon Husted to create an AI and tech talent team within the U.S. Office of Personnel Management. He was born on July 12, 1982, in Boston, Massachusetts, to South Korean immigrant parents and is the first Korean American elected to the U.S. Senate.

name: Jim Risch
notes: Jim Risch is a U.S. Senator from Idaho who has served since 2009 and is a member of the Republican Party. He serves on five key committees: Energy and Natural Resources, Foreign Relations, Small Business and Entrepreneurship, Intelligence, and Ethics, and serves as Chairman of the Senate Foreign Relations Committee. He previously served as Idaho's 31st Governor in 2006 and as Lieutenant Governor, and achieved major tax reform as Governor, cutting property taxes by 20%. He is consistently recognized as one of the most conservative members of the U.S. Senate.

name: Ben Horowitz
notes: Ben Horowitz is a cofounder and general partner at the venture capital firm Andreessen Horowitz. He previously co-founded and served as president and chief executive officer of the enterprise software company Opsware, which Hewlett-Packard acquired in 2007. Andreessen Horowitz recently raised more than $15 billion across five new funds, with Horowitz stating that at this moment of profound technological opportunity, it is fundamentally important for humanity that America wins. He has authored books including The Hard Thing About Hard Things: Building a Business When There Are No Easy Answers.

name: Donald Trump
notes: Donald Trump served as the 45th President of the United States from 2017 to 2021 and was inaugurated as the 47th president on January 20, 2025. On March 20, 2026, the Trump Administration released a National AI Legislative Framework that addresses six key objectives including protecting children, safeguarding American communities, and ensuring American AI dominance. In December 2025, President Trump signed an Executive Order to protect American AI innovation from inconsistent state laws and directed the Attorney General to establish an AI Litigation Task Force. Trump has appointed David Sacks as White House AI 'Czar' who has called the administration's AI policy the '5 Cs': child safety, communities, creators, censorship, and competitiveness.

name: Coinbase
notes: Coinbase is joining the x402 Foundation alongside tech titans like Amazon, Google, and Stripe. Coinbase has launched crypto wallet infrastructure that allows AI agents to spend, earn and trade crypto. The company's mission is to increase economic freedom for more than 1 billion people by providing a trusted platform for crypto assets. Coinbase delivered strong Q4 and full year 2024 results, with full year revenue of $6.6 billion, net income of $2.6 billion, and Adjusted EBITDA of $3.3 billion.

name: John Kennedy
notes: Sen. John Kennedy (R-LA) questioned David Evan Harris about AI regulation during a Senate Judiciary Committee hearing on September 17, 2024. Kennedy co-introduced the AI Labeling Act (S. 2691) with Sen. Brian Schatz (D-HI) on October 24, 2023, which would notify consumers when they interact with AI-generated content and require developers to disclose such content. Kennedy has introduced legislation to withhold senators' pay during government shutdowns, with the U.S. Senate Rules Committee unanimously advancing this proposal in December 2025. Kennedy reacted positively to President Trump's joint address to Congress in March 2025, stating "President Trump has given us a blueprint".

name: Cory Gardner
notes: Cory Gardner is President & CEO of NCTA – The Internet & Television Association, beginning his tenure on September 22, 2025. He previously served as a U.S. Senator from Colorado from 2015 to 2021 and as a U.S. Representative for Colorado's 4th Congressional District from 2011 to 2015. Gardner served as chair of the National Republican Senatorial Committee from 2017 to 2019. He was consistently ranked among the most bipartisan senators, including being ranked the 5th most bipartisan senator of the 115th Congress in March 2019.

name: Senate Commerce, Science, and Transportation Committee
notes: The Senate Commerce, Science, and Transportation Committee is a standing committee of the United States Senate composed of 28 Senators and led by Committee Chairman Ted Cruz (R-Texas) and Ranking Member Maria Cantwell (D-Wash.). The committee has broad jurisdiction over all matters concerning interstate commerce, science and technology policy, and transportation, and is one of the largest of the Senate's standing committees. The committee has six subcommittees which together oversee issues ranging from communications, highways, aviation, rail, shipping, transportation security, merchant marine, the Coast Guard, oceans, fisheries, weather, disasters, science, space, interstate commerce, tourism, consumer issues, economic development, technology, competitiveness, product safety, and insurance. The committee passed the bipartisan Future of AI Innovation Act to maintain United States leadership in the global race to develop artificial intelligence (AI) and other emerging technologies.

name: Special Competitive Studies Project
notes: The Special Competitive Studies Project (SCSP) is a bipartisan, non-profit initiative with a mission to make recommendations to strengthen America's long-term competitiveness as artificial intelligence (AI) and other emerging technologies reshape national security, economy, and society. SCSP carries forward the vital mission of the National Security Commission on Artificial Intelligence (NSCAI), which was established in 2018. The organization was founded in 2021 and has 98 employees, located in Crystal City, United States. SCSP has launched multiple commissions and task forces, including collaborations with NVIDIA on AI and the future of work, and commissions on fusion energy and robotics for advanced manufacturing.

name: Berkeley Artificial Intelligence Research
notes: The Berkeley Artificial Intelligence Research (BAIR) Lab brings together UC Berkeley researchers across the areas of computer vision, machine learning, natural language processing, planning, and robotics. BAIR includes over two dozen faculty and more than a hundred graduate students pursuing research on fundamental advances in the above areas as well as cross-cutting themes including multi-modal deep learning, human-compatible AI, and connecting AI with other scientific disciplines and the humanities. The lab was established in 1990 as part of the University of California, Berkeley. BAIR operates the Open Research Commons (ORC) which brings together academia and industry to cultivate an open, interdisciplinary research environment.

name: Berkeley Institute for Data Science
notes: The Berkeley Institute for Data Science (BIDS) is a central hub of data-intensive research, open source software, and data science training programs at UC Berkeley that launched in 2013. BIDS is an integral part of UC Berkeley's College of Computing, Data Science, and Society (CDSS) and was made possible by grants from the Gordon and Betty Moore Foundation and the Alfred P. Sloan Foundation. The Institute is one of three Moore-Sloan Data Science Environments along with data science organizations at the University of Washington and New York University. BIDS is currently led by Fernando Pérez as Faculty Director.

name: Responsible AI Initiative
notes: Multiple organizations operate under the name "Responsible AI Initiative" with different scopes and affiliations. The University of Utah operates the One-U Responsible AI Initiative, which aims to advance AI to achieve societal good while protecting privacy, civil rights, and civil liberties. Emory University launched a Responsible AI website as part of their enterprise-wide Responsible AI initiative, led by the Emory AI Group with input from the Responsible AI Executive Governance Committee. Carnegie Mellon University launched a Responsible AI initiative out of the Block Center for Technology and Society with support from faculty in the School of Computer Science.

name: Université de Montréal
notes: Université de Montréal is a French-language institution of higher learning founded in 1878, regarded as one of the most important universities in Canada in terms of research and one of the largest by enrolment. The university has 16 faculties and two affiliated schools (Polytechnique Montréal and HEC Montréal), with an enrollment of nearly 67,600 students in over 600 programs. UdeM ranked 9th globally in Artificial Intelligence research according to the Shanghai Academic Ranking of World Universities in 2026. The university has an annual revenue of $212.0M and employs 7,578 people.

name: Mila - Quebec Artificial Intelligence Institute
notes: Mila - Quebec Artificial Intelligence Institute is the world's largest academic AI research center with over 1,400 researchers and 140+ professors. Founded in 1993 by Yoshua Bengio at Université de Montréal, it is an independent nonprofit research organization dedicated to advancing artificial intelligence through fundamental research and talent development. The institute is recognized worldwide for its major contributions to AI, particularly in deep learning, language modeling, machine translation, object recognition and generative models. Mila is part of the Pan-Canadian Artificial Intelligence Strategy alongside Alberta's Amii and Toronto's Vector Institute.

name: Element AI
notes: Element AI was a Canadian multinational artificial intelligence company based in Montreal, Quebec that was founded in October 2016. The company was funded by the Government of Canada for CAD$5 million, and raised US$102 million independently, before being acquired by ServiceNow. ServiceNow announced it has signed an agreement to acquire Element AI on November 30, 2020, with ServiceNow completing the acquisition of Element AI on January 8, 2021. The company was running out of money and options and sold for US $230 million.

name: Ian Goodfellow
notes: Ian J. Goodfellow (born 1987) is an American computer scientist, engineer, and executive, most noted for his work on artificial neural networks and deep learning. He is a research scientist at Google DeepMind, was previously employed as a research scientist at Google Brain and director of machine learning at Apple as well as one of the first employees at OpenAI. He is best known for inventing generative adversarial networks (GANs), using deep learning to generate images. He co-wrote, as the first author, the textbook Deep Learning (2016) and wrote the chapter on deep learning in the authoritative textbook of the field of artificial intelligence, Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach.

name: McGill University
notes: McGill University was founded in 1821 from a generous bequest by James McGill, a prominent Scottish merchant. The university offers an Applied Artificial Intelligence Minor in the Faculty of Engineering that provides foundation for applying AI techniques in various fields. McGill has several AI-related organizations including the McGill Centre for Intelligent Machines, which has been a world leader in research on intelligent systems since 1985. The McGill Collaborative for AI and Society (McCAIS) aims to understand the impact of AI on society and develop evidence-based best practices for its responsible use.

name: International Association for Safe and Ethical AI
notes: The International Association for Safe and Ethical AI (IASEAI) is a non-profit organization founded in 2024 with headquarters in Paris, France. The organization's stated mission is to address the risks and opportunities associated with advances in artificial intelligence and to ensure that AI systems operate safely and ethically, benefiting all of humanity. IASEAI held its inaugural conference, IASEAI '25, on February 6–7, 2025 in Paris, which brought together experts from academia, civil society, industry, and government to discuss developments in AI safety and ethics. The organization is led by Stuart Russell as President and Mark Nitzberg as Interim Executive Director.

name: National Republican Senatorial Committee
notes: The National Republican Senatorial Committee (NRSC) is the Republican Hill committee for the United States Senate, working to elect Republicans to the Senate. The NRSC was founded in 1916 as the Republican Senatorial Campaign Committee and was reorganized in 1948 and renamed the National Republican Senatorial Committee. It has been chaired by Senator Tim Scott from South Carolina since 2025. The NRSC raised $88 million in 2025, which is the best off-year fundraising for the Committee since 2021.

name: Katie Britt
notes: Katie Britt is a U.S. Senator from Alabama serving since 2023, and is a member of the Republican Party. She is calling for guardrails on artificial intelligence chatbots because of the potential to harm minors. Britt has two teenagers, ages 15 and 16, and draws on her experience as a mother in her advocacy work. She previously served as president and CEO of the Business Council of Alabama from 2019 to 2021, and as chief of staff for Senator Richard Shelby from 2016.

name: Billy Perrigo
notes: Correspondent at TIME magazine based in London, covering the tech industry with a focus on AI and social media. Known for investigative reporting on AI labor exploitation: broke the story that OpenAI used outsourced Kenyan workers earning less than $2/hour to detoxify ChatGPT. Also reported that OpenAI lobbied to water down the EU AI Act. His investigation "Inside Facebook's African Sweatshop" was a finalist for the 2022 Orwell Prize. Reporting focuses on how tech companies restructure power, with attention to impacts on marginalized communities, workers, and postcolonial states.

name: Marc Benioff
notes: CEO of Salesforce and owner of TIME magazine. Major tech industry figure who has been increasingly vocal about AI regulation. At Davos 2026, called AI models "suicide coaches" and demanded reform of Section 230 to hold AI companies accountable for harms. Framed AI safety as a values question: "What's more important to us, growth or our kids?" Salesforce has launched Agentforce (enterprise AI agents) while Benioff simultaneously pushes for guardrails. Consistent pattern of calling for tech regulation — made similar calls about social media at Davos 2018. Advocates for responsible AI deployment with meaningful safety requirements.

name: Stanford Artificial Intelligence Laboratory (SAIL)
notes: One of the oldest AI research labs, founded at Stanford in 1962 by John McCarthy. Research spans NLP, computer vision, robotics, reinforcement learning, and computational biomedicine. In 2025, Carlos Guestrin became Director and SAIL formally joined forces with Stanford HAI, integrating safety and policy work alongside core capabilities research.

name: Stanford Center for AI Safety
notes: Stanford University research center founded in 2018 by Clark Barrett, David Dill, Mykel Kochenderfer, and Dorsa Sadigh, operating as an industrial affiliates program. Conducts research in formal verification, robust decision-making, and trustworthy AI; distinct from the independent nonprofit Center for AI Safety (safe.ai) run by Dan Hendrycks. Focus spans technical safety, fairness, accountability, and policy.

name: MIT Data to AI Lab
notes: MIT research group within the Laboratory for Information and Decision Systems (LIDS), founded in 2015 by Principal Research Scientist Kalyan Veeramachaneni. Develops large-scale AI and ML systems that work alongside humans in applications spanning healthcare, finance, education, and energy. Notable for the Synthetic Data Vault open-source project, spun out commercially as DataCebo.

name: DAF-MIT AI Accelerator
notes: Joint partnership between the Department of the Air Force (DAF) and MIT to advance AI research for defense and civilian applications. Established 2019; renewed for another five years in 2024. Combines military personnel (embedded officers and enlisted Airmen) with MIT faculty, researchers, and students. Has launched 20+ projects engaging 150+ researchers across MIT campus and MIT Lincoln Laboratory. Focus areas include rapid prototyping, scaling, and ethical application of AI algorithms for national security. Leverages universities, small businesses, traditional defense contractors, and non-traditional commercial ventures.

name: MIT Schwarzman College of Computing
notes: MIT's cross-disciplinary computing college founded in 2018 with a $1B endowment anchored by a $350M gift from Stephen Schwarzman. Led by founding dean Daniel Huttenlocher, it integrates AI research across all MIT departments. Hosts the MIT AI Policy Forum and publishes governance-focused policy briefs; advocates for technically-informed, sector-specific AI regulation.

name: Kempner Institute
notes: Harvard University-wide institute for the study of natural and artificial intelligence, founded 2022 with $500M gift from Mark Zuckerberg and Priscilla Chan (Chan Zuckerberg Initiative). Co-directed by Sham Kakade (CS) and Bernardo Sabatini (neurobiology). Operates the Kempner AI Cluster at MGHPCC (1,144 H100 GPUs as of March 2026, ranked 85th on TOP500 in 2024). Research spans mathematical models of intelligence, cognitive theories, neurobiological basis of intelligence, and AI applications in science. Supports undergraduate research (KURE program), postdocs, and 10 new faculty lines. Sits at the intersection of neuroscience and AI, seeking fundamental principles underlying both human and machine intelligence.

name: Massachusetts Green High Performance Computing Center
notes: Intercollegiate high-performance computing facility in Holyoke, Massachusetts, jointly operated by Boston University, Harvard, MIT, Northeastern, UMass, and Yale. LEED Platinum certified; powered by Holyoke Gas & Electric with >90% carbon-free energy (>70% local hydro and solar). Hosts hundreds of thousands of CPUs, millions of GPU cores, >50PB storage. Announced $16M expansion in Oct 2024 to build a quantum computing complex. Houses the Kempner AI Cluster for Harvard. Connected to Holyoke's municipal fiber grid and numerous campus, regional, and national networks.

name: Sanjeev Arora
notes: Charles C. Fitzmorris Professor of Computer Science at Princeton and founding director of Princeton Language and Intelligence (PLI). Indian-American theoretical computer scientist (PhD UC Berkeley, joined Princeton 1994). Research focuses on mathematical understanding of deep learning — optimization, generalization, interpretability — to enable better and safer AI. Member of the National Academy of Sciences and American Academy of Arts and Sciences. Awards include ACM Prize in Computing (2011), Fulkerson Prize (2012), Gödel Prize (2001, 2010), Packard Fellowship (1997), ACM Doctoral Dissertation Award (1995). Fellow of the ACM.

name: Arthur Spirling
notes: Class of 1987 Professor of Politics at Princeton and Director of the Center for Statistics and Machine Learning. Previously on faculty at Harvard and NYU. PhD University of Rochester; BA/MA London School of Economics. Research centers on quantitative methods for political behavior, text-as-data, and problems at the intersection of data science and social science. Has published on LLMs in politics (e.g. 'Large Language Models Can Argue in Convincing Ways About Politics, But Humans Dislike AI Authors'), the ethics of using proprietary LLMs in academic research (Nature Computational Science 2023), and advocacy for open generative AI models in science (Nature 2023).

name: Olga Troyanskaya
notes: Maduraperuma/Khot Professor of Computer Science at Princeton; director of Princeton Precision Health. Also deputy director of Genomics at the Flatiron Institute (Simons Foundation) since 2014. PhD in Biomedical Informatics from Stanford. Research builds AI methods to decode genomes and diagnose/treat complex diseases, focusing on the non-coding genome and multi-omics modeling. Affiliated with Lewis-Sigler Institute for Integrative Genomics, Dept of Molecular Biology, and Omenn-Darling Bioengineering Institute. Awards: MIT Technology Review Top Young Innovator, Sloan Research Fellowship, NSF CAREER award, ISCB Overton Award, Genetics Society of America Herskowitz Award.

name: Tom Griffiths
notes: Henry R. Luce Professor of Information Technology, Consciousness, and Culture at Princeton, jointly appointed in Psychology and Computer Science. Director of the Computational Cognitive Science Lab (CoCoSci) and inaugural director of the Princeton Laboratory for Artificial Intelligence (AI Lab). PhD Psychology from Stanford (2005); previously at Brown and UC Berkeley (full professor 2015, director of Berkeley's Institute of Cognitive and Brain Sciences). Research applies mathematical modeling and computational methods to understand human cognition, and uses psychological concepts to interpret AI systems. Co-authored 'Algorithms to Live By' (2016) with Brian Christian.

name: The Princeton Laboratory for Artificial Intelligence
notes: Princeton's AI Lab supports and expands AI research across the university with a novel organizational model. Directed by Tom Griffiths; associate director Olga Russakovsky. Supports three research initiatives: Princeton Language and Intelligence (large AI models, led by Sanjeev Arora), AI for Accelerating Invention (AI^2, AI in science/engineering), and Natural and Artificial Minds (NAM, human-AI interrelationship). Shared infrastructure includes admin staff, AI Postdoctoral Fellows, research software engineers, data scientists, and a compute cluster with 300 H100 GPUs. Faculty span natural sciences, engineering, social sciences, and humanities.

name: ai@cam
notes: University of Cambridge flagship mission on AI, led by Neil Lawrence (DeepMind Professor of Machine Learning). Aims to develop AI-enabled innovation that benefits science, citizens, and society by leveraging Cambridge's interdisciplinary research community. Focuses on responsible AI development requiring expertise from research, business, policy, civil society, and affected communities. Engages in public dialogue on AI governance; research has shown public desire for AI systems with meaningful human oversight, transparency, accountability, and protection from harms.

name: Neil Lawrence
notes: DeepMind Professor of Machine Learning at the University of Cambridge; leads ai@cam, Cambridge's flagship AI mission. Senior AI Fellow at the Alan Turing Institute; visiting professor at University of Sheffield. Previously Director of Machine Learning at Amazon (3 years). Author of 'The Atomic Human: Understanding Ourselves in the Age of AI.' Has given parliamentary testimony on AI regulation. Advocates strongly for public-interest AI governance: warns against regulation that serves 'the digital oligarchy,' cautions about 'sowing the seeds of 10,000 Horizon scandals,' and emphasizes data transparency and privacy. Research focuses on interaction of ML with the physical world, originally motivated by ML deployment in Africa.

name: Adrian Weller
notes: Director of Research in Machine Learning at the University of Cambridge; Programme Director for Trust and Society at the Leverhulme Centre for the Future of Intelligence (CFI). MBE. Turing AI Fellow in Trustworthy Machine Learning; heads Safe and Ethical AI at The Alan Turing Institute. Co-director of ELLIS programme on Human-centric Machine Learning. Member of UNESCO Ad Hoc Expert Group on Ethics of AI and IEEE Standards Working Group on Explainable AI. Emphasizes academic engagement with policymakers for 'technically and legally sensible' policy. Research spans interpretability, fairness, privacy, safety, ethics. Has written that humans cannot escape accountability for AI decisions and warns of major labor displacement ahead.

name: Saïd Business School
notes: Business school of the University of Oxford. Offers a portfolio of AI-focused executive education programs: Oxford Artificial Intelligence Programme, Oxford Executive Diploma in AI for Business (master's-level), Oxford Leading AI Implementation Programme, and Oxford Generative and Agentic AI Programme (directed by Matthias Holweg and Mike Wooldridge). Draws on Oxford's research in multi-agent architectures, operational design, and responsible AI practice. Focused on business leaders understanding AI workings, possibilities, ethics, and implementation strategy.

name: Kyunghyun Cho
notes: Glen de Vries Professor of Health Statistics and professor of Computer Science and Data Science at NYU. Co-head of the Global AI Frontier Lab at NYU with Yann LeCun (2024). PhD from Aalto University (2014). Co-authored the 2014 paper with Bahdanau and Bengio introducing neural machine translation and attention mechanisms. Previously research scientist at Facebook AI Research (2017-2020); co-founded Prescient Design (acquired by Genentech 2021), served as Executive Director of Frontier Research until Jan 2026. Has publicly questioned existential AI risk framing, arguing focus should be on concrete current-application risks (healthcare, military). Critical of alarmist narratives distracting from practical regulation.

name: Global AI Frontier Lab
notes: Joint U.S.-South Korea AI research center at NYU, established May 2024 via MOU between NYU and Korea's IITP. Co-directed by Yann LeCun and Kyunghyun Cho. Focuses on three pillars: fundamental AI research, trustworthy/responsible AI, and AI for healthcare. Korean government investing ~$34M (45B won) with NYU contributing ~$31M by 2028. Opened September 2024 at One Metrotech Center, Brooklyn.

name: Institute for Information & Communications Technology Planning & Evaluation
notes: South Korean government institution under the Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT) that manages national ICT R&D policy, funding, and human resource development. Key funder of the Global AI Frontier Lab at NYU. Restructured in 2026 around "AI Transformation (AX)" with new teams for Physical AI, AI Foundation (data), and AI Safety and Trust. Manages program areas in AI, AI semiconductors, AX convergence, and physical AI.

name: Senate Committee on Commerce, Science and Transportation
notes: Key Senate committee with primary jurisdiction over AI legislation, chaired by Ted Cruz (R-TX) since 2025. Cruz unveiled the SANDBOX Act in September 2025 and a five-pillar AI policy framework explicitly advocating a light-touch, pro-innovation regulatory approach to maintain U.S. leadership over China.

name: House Bipartisan Task Force on Artificial Intelligence
notes: Bipartisan U.S. House task force created in Feb 2024 by Speaker Johnson and Leader Jeffries, co-chaired by Reps. Obernolte and Lieu. Released a 273-page final report in Dec 2024 with 66 findings and 89 recommendations across 15 policy areas. Sunset at end of 118th Congress; no successor body established in the 119th Congress as of early 2025.

name: House Committee on Science Space & Tech
notes: U.S. House committee with primary jurisdiction over AI R&D policy, NIST AI standards, and NSF AI programs. Passed nine bipartisan AI bills in Sept 2024 including the CREATE AI Act (NAIRR), NSF AI Education Act, and Workforce for AI Trust Act. Contributed to the Bipartisan AI Task Force report (Dec 2024) with 66 findings and 89 recommendations. Held "Advancing America's AI Action Plan" hearings in 2025-26 with OSTP Director Michael Kratsios. Chaired by Jay Obernolte (R-CA) in 119th Congress.

name: Education and Workforce Committee
notes: U.S. House committee with jurisdiction over labor, education, and workforce training policy as it intersects with AI. Launched "Building an AI-Ready America" hearing series in Jan 2026 covering AI in the workplace, workplace safety, AI in education/teaching, and employer-led AI training. Examining AI's impact on hiring, gig work, and worker management. Key forum for workforce displacement and AI readiness policy.

name: Jerry McNerney
notes: Former U.S. Representative (CA-9, 2007-2023) who chaired the Congressional AI Caucus and authored the AI in Government Act (2020). Transitioned to California State Senate District 5 in Dec 2024. Authored SB 813, which creates the CA Artificial Intelligence Standards and Safety Commission with independent verification organizations for AI safety standards. Previously a Fellow at Stanford HAI and Senior Policy Advisor at Pillsbury Winthrop Shaw Pittman. Also affiliated with the AI Trust Foundation. Math PhD; one of few STEM-trained members of the legislature.

name: Thomas Umberg
notes: California State Senator (D-Santa Ana, SD-34) and Chair of the Senate Judiciary Committee, which is the key committee for AI regulation bills in the CA legislature. Authored SB 574 restricting lawyers' and arbitrators' use of AI (requiring human review, banning delegation of decisions to AI, protecting confidential data). Publicly opposed Trump executive order to preempt state AI regulation. As Judiciary Chair, shapes which AI bills advance through the legislature, including SB 1047 (Wiener) and other safety bills.

name: Steve Padilla
notes: California State Senator (D-San Diego, SD-18) and leading voice on AI consumer protection, especially for minors. Authored SB 243, the nation's first chatbot safeguards law (signed by Newsom), requiring operators to implement safeguards against sexual content for minors and disclosure that chatbots are AI. Also authored SB 420 (California AI Bill of Rights framework), SB 903 (protecting against AI therapy products), and proposed a 4-year moratorium on AI chatbots in children's toys. Publicly criticized OpenAI for lobbying against chatbot protections.

name: International Trade Administration
notes: Bureau within U.S. Dept of Commerce responsible for trade promotion and commercial diplomacy. Leads the American AI Exports Program (per Trump July 2025 EO) to promote export of U.S. AI technology stack globally. Establishing National AI Center as gateway for U.S. AI exports. Has a dedicated AI Team in the U.S. Commercial Service. Accepting proposals from industry consortia (April-June 2026) for full-stack AI technology packages to international partners. Led by Under Secretary William Kimmitt.

name: William Kimmitt
notes: 17th Under Secretary of Commerce for International Trade, sworn in Aug 2025. Leads ITA and serves as principal trade adviser to Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick. Drives the American AI Exports Program, including leading ITA delegation to India AI Impact Summit 2026 in Bangalore/New Delhi. Previously a partner at Kirkland & Ellis LLP (complex litigation, trade/investment advisory). BA from Duke, JD from Georgetown Law.

name: U.S. Department of State
notes: U.S. foreign affairs agency with growing AI diplomacy role. Released first Enterprise AI Strategy FY 2024-2025 under Sec. Blinken. Key AI-relevant offices: Office of Special Envoy for Critical and Emerging Technology (S/TECH) for strategic tech competition and allied coordination; Bureau for Cyberspace and Digital Policy (CDP) for AI governance norms. Launched Partnership for Global Inclusivity on AI (PGIAI, Sept 2024) with major AI companies. Deploying internal AI tools including StateChat and agentic AI pilots. March 2026 EO by Newsom is state-level; this is federal.

name: Government Operations Agency
notes: California state agency (GovOps) overseeing CDT, Office of Data and Innovation, DGS, and CalHR. Leads GenAI implementation across CA state government per Newsom's 2023 executive order. Spearheading CalCompute, a public computing cluster for safe/ethical AI research. Coordinating AI procurement standards under March 2026 EO (N-5-26) requiring vendor trust/safety certifications. Houses the State Technology Council and AI Community of Practice for sharing AI best practices across departments.

name: California Department of General Services
notes: California state department under GovOps responsible for procurement, real estate, and administrative services. Key role in AI governance: directed by March 2026 EO (N-5-26) to develop new vendor certification requirements for AI companies seeking state contracts, requiring attestations on content safety (CSAM prevention), bias mitigation, and civil rights/liberties protections. Works with CDT to set AI procurement standards and facilitate employee access to vetted GenAI tools. Recommendations for new AI vendor certifications due within 120 days of EO.

name: California Department of Technology
notes: California's central IT agency (CDT), oversees state technology policy and services. Leads AI integration for state government under Gov. Newsom's executive orders (EO N-12-23). Launched 'Poppy,' a GenAI digital assistant for state employees built on large language models, with 20+ state departments testing it. Collaborates with Office of Data and Innovation (ODI) and CalHR on AI workforce training. Implements the state's Acceptable Use of AI policy for government agencies. California enacted 18 AI-related laws in 2024 and signed SB 53 (Transparency in Frontier AI Act) in 2025. In March 2026, Newsom signed a first-of-its-kind executive order strengthening AI protections as the Trump administration rolled back federal guardrails.

name: California Department of Human Resources
notes: CalHR manages California's state workforce (civil service rules, benefits, training). Partnered with the Office of Data and Innovation (ODI) to launch the Foundations of GenAI Certificate series, a free training pathway for state employees to develop responsible GenAI skills. Part of a multi-agency AI workforce effort directed by Gov. Newsom's EO N-12-23, alongside CDT and GovOps. Also involved in creating the Chief Data and AI Officer position at Caltrans. California's Civil Rights Council approved regulations (effective Oct 2025) protecting against AI-related employment discrimination.

name: NYS Office of Information Technology Services
notes: New York State's central IT agency (ITS). Authored the state's 'Acceptable Use of Artificial Intelligence Technologies' policy (Jan 2024) under Gov. Hochul's direction, requiring leadership approval for AI systems, human oversight for automated decisions, and NIST AI RMF-based risk assessments. Appointed Eleonore Fournier-Tombs as Chief AI Officer to refine policy. Part of the Empire AI initiative. A 2025 State Comptroller audit found gaps: the policy lacks detailed implementation guidance and ITS has no inventory of AI systems across state agencies. Delivered an AI training tool for the state workforce.

name: Ohio Department of Job and Family Services
notes: Ohio DJFS administers unemployment insurance, workforce development, and public assistance programs. Won the 2025 Merrill Baumgardner Award from the National Association of State Workforce Agencies for AI innovation in unemployment services. Deployed four AI tools: a multilingual virtual assistant for unemployment claims (15 languages), intelligent document processing for classification/indexing, a call center AI bot, and a GenAI tool simplifying complex policy manuals. Part of Ohio's broader AI strategy coordinated through InnovateOhio. Also involved in the OhioMeansJobs AI-powered job matching platform.

name: Matt Damschroder
notes: Director of the Ohio Department of Job and Family Services (ODJFS) since July 2021, appointed by Gov. Mike DeWine. Previously served as Director of the Ohio Department of Administrative Services (DAS) from Jan 2019, where he oversaw the Office of Information Technology and InnovateOhio Platform. Under his leadership, ODJFS won the 2025 Merrill Baumgardner Award for AI innovation in unemployment services, deploying multilingual virtual assistants, intelligent document processing, call center AI, and GenAI policy simplification tools. Advocates 'responsible AI' focused on user experience over bureaucracy. Republican appointee in the DeWine-Husted administration.

name: Ron DeSantis
notes: Governor of Florida (Republican). Proposed an AI Bill of Rights for Florida citizens covering data privacy, parental controls, consumer protections, and restrictions on AI use of individuals' name/image/likeness without consent. Also proposed protections against hyperscale AI data center costs and empowering local governments to reject their development. Notably pushed back against Trump's Dec 2025 executive order seeking to preempt state AI regulation, asserting 'We have a right to do this.' Claims Florida's proposed rules are consistent with federal goals on child safety, data center oversight, and procurement. Represents a state-level regulatory stance that is more protective than the federal approach despite party alignment.

name: Spencer Cox
notes: Governor of Utah (Republican). Leading advocate for 'pro-human AI' policy at the state level. Announced $10M initiative for AI workforce readiness at the Utah AI Summit (Dec 2025). Utah created an Office of Artificial Intelligence Policy in 2023. Pushed back strongly against Trump's Dec 2025 executive order preempting state AI regulation, asserting states should lead on AI policy. Proposed legislation to reduce AI chatbot harms, require transparency for AI-generated deepfakes, restrain AI in healthcare, and create a state framework requiring frontier AI developers to disclose safety protocols and child protection measures with whistleblower protections. Has gained national recognition as a Republican governor willing to regulate AI despite federal pushback.

name: Trump administration
notes: The Trump administration (second term, 2025-) has pursued an aggressive pro-innovation, deregulatory AI agenda. Day 1: revoked Biden's AI EO 14110 (EO 14148, Jan 20 2025). EO 14179 (Jan 23 2025) 'Removing Barriers to American Leadership in AI' launched an action plan for global AI dominance. OSTP released 90-point AI Action Plan (July 2025) with three pillars: Accelerating Innovation, Building AI Infrastructure, Leading International Diplomacy/Security. Dec 2025 EO 'Ensuring a National Policy Framework for AI' attempted to preempt state AI regulation, threatening to withhold federal funds from states with 'conflicting' AI laws. Drew pushback from Republican governors (DeSantis, Cox). Cross-cutting priorities: protecting workers, ensuring trustworthy/unbiased AI, safeguarding against misuse. DOGE (Department of Government Efficiency) pursuing separate AI-for-government-reform agenda.
